---
title: "Session 3 exercise: reproducibility and project workflow"
subtitle: "ZAS Pythonistas working group"
date: today
date-format: "MMMM YYYY"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    highlight-style: github
    theme: flatly
jupyter: python3
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
---

## Before you start

Make sure you have:

- Cloned the group repo and opened it as a folder in Positron or VS Code
- Activated your conda environment (`zas_python_wg`) -- check the bottom status bar
- Opened a terminal and verified with `conda info --envs` (asterisk = active)

Render this document when you are done: `quarto render session03_exercise.qmd`

---

## Part A: check your environment

### A1. In the terminal, list your conda environments and confirm the right one is active:

```{.bash filename="Terminal"}
conda info --envs
```

### A2. Export your full environment and open the file to inspect it:

```{.bash filename="Terminal"}
conda env export > environment-full.yml
```

### A3. Now export only what you explicitly installed:

```{.bash filename="Terminal"}
conda env export --from-history > environment-minimal.yml
```

What is the difference between the two files? Which would you commit to the repo and why?

### A4. Restart session

Close and re-open your IDE (VS Code/Positron). Open your project folder, activate conda your environment in the terminal (`conda activate env_name`), and select the relevant interpreter.

---

## Part B: fix the broken file path

The cell below contains a hardcoded absolute path that will break on every
machine except the one it was written on.

### B1. Run the cell as-is and read the error.

```{python}
import pandas as pd
from pathlib import Path

# TODO B2: this path is broken -- fix it using pyprojroot or pathlib
# See the session handout for the pattern
data_path = "/Users/replace-me/projects/zas-pythonistas/data/raw/sample.csv"

df = pd.read_csv(data_path)
print(f"Loaded {len(df)} rows")
df.head()
```

### B2. Fix the path using `pyprojroot`:

```{python}
# Your fixed version here

```

### B3. Once it works, notice how the output renders inline in the document.
This is one advantage of Quarto over a plain script.


### B4. Update environment.yml

 Save your environment to environment.yml. Inspect it; is pyprojroot there?

---

## Part C: git hygiene

Work through these steps in the terminal -- you cannot run git commands
in a Python chunk, but you can document what you did here.

### C1. Check what git currently sees:

```{.bash filename="Terminal"}
git status
```

Do you see any files that should not be committed (e.g. `__pycache__/`, `.ipynb_checkpoints/`)?

### C2. Create or update `.gitignore` to exclude:

```{.bash filename=".gitignore"}
__pycache__/
*.pyc
.ipynb_checkpoints/
.venv/
environment-full.yml
environment-minimal.yml
```

### C3. Stage and commit your work:

```{.bash filename="Terminal"}
git add session03_exercise.qmd .gitignore
git commit -m "add: session 3 exercise and updated gitignore"
```

Write your commit message below so you have a record of what you did:

> My commit message: ...

---

## Part D: compare formats (discussion)

Now that you have worked in this `.qmd` file, compare the experience
with the plain `.py` script version (`session03_exercise.py`).

Think about:

- Where did the output appear in each format?
- Which was easier to navigate?
- Which would you share with a collaborator?
- What does `Kernel -> Restart & Run All` do differently here vs in a `.ipynb` notebook?

```{python}
# You can use this cell for any extra exploration

```

---

## Stretch: render to HTML

If you have not already, render this document:

```{.bash filename="Terminal"}
quarto render session03_exercise.qmd
```

Open the resulting `.html` file. Notice that:

- Code and output appear together
- The document is self-contained and shareable
- The path fix from Part B is visible and reproducible
